position_summary: Claude's position treats the plan as near-complete, but several load-bearing claims are either unverified on this machine or contradicted by the live access path available to the reviewer. The biggest risks are not storage micro-optimization; they are whether WSL/Claude Science can actually be launched on this managed host, whether the install source/product entitlement is real and current, and whether the project should be switching from the stated Builder-track brief to Researcher-track at all.

findings.title: WSL access is denied from the available Windows context, so the install path may be blocked before step 1
findings.evidence: Read-only probe `wsl -l -v` returned `Access is denied. Error code: Wsl/EnumerateDistros/Service/E_ACCESSDENIED`; `wsl -d Ubuntu -- lsb_release -a`, `uname -r`, and prereq probes all returned `Wsl/Service/E_ACCESSDENIED`. Artifact sections 2 and 5 assume `wsl -d Ubuntu` and in-distro apt commands are available.
findings.concern_path: On a VCU-managed machine, if the same policy boundary applies to the user workflow, no Ubuntu shell can be entered, no apt prereqs can be installed, no GPU smoke test can run, and the entire 6-day plan stalls on endpoint policy rather than Claude Science setup.
findings.suggested_action: Before debating placement, prove the target user can run `wsl -l -v` and enter `Ubuntu`; if not, escalate managed-device policy or move to an approved host.

findings.title: Claude Science install source and product availability are unproven, making the critical-path premise brittle
findings.evidence: Artifact R4 admits the script URL came from an indexed/beta doc and must be re-read; web searches for official public docs using `site:docs.anthropic.com Claude Science WSL install bubblewrap socat`, `site:claude.com/docs/claude-science run on Windows WSL install`, and `claude-science serve --detached` returned no results in this environment.
findings.concern_path: If the beta docs, installer URL, CLI name, flags, or entitlement changed, the plan fails after investing time in WSL/storage preparation. Worse, a stale `curl | bash` command could install the wrong artifact or nothing at all.
findings.suggested_action: Make official current Claude Science docs and installer command verification a gate before any host changes or data movement.

findings.title: The project brief says Builder-track, but the plan silently pivots the deliverable to Researcher-track
findings.evidence: AGENTS.md says `This project is a hackathon Builder-track entry: PyZoBot Arbiter`; artifact section 1 says `Track chosen: Researcher` and `Claude Science is required tooling for the Researcher track`.
findings.concern_path: The team may optimize for the wrong judging rubric, spend scarce time installing a research workbench, and underinvest in the Builder-track app/DTO/tool contract already described as the live project direction.
findings.suggested_action: Resolve track ownership explicitly before treating Claude Science as critical path.

findings.title: The plan still gives a blind `curl | bash` exact install step despite recognizing URL drift and managed-machine risk
findings.evidence: Artifact section 5 says `curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install-claude-science.sh | bash`; R4 separately says inspect the script rather than blind piping; open question 4 acknowledges VCU/CrowdStrike/KACE/Defender interference.
findings.concern_path: A managed endpoint can block, quarantine, or partially allow an opaque shell installer, leaving a corrupted user profile or broken CLI. The plan also lacks hash/signature verification, audit capture, or an offline rollback point for profile mutations.
findings.suggested_action: Replace the exact step with fetch-inspect-verify-run, record installer contents/version, and pre-check managed endpoint policy.

findings.title: GPU readiness is overstated because `nvidia-smi` is not evidence that Claude Science sandboxed tasks can run CUDA
findings.evidence: Artifact section 2 treats `nvidia-smi inside WSL succeeds` as GPU-in-WSL proof; section 7 R6 admits distro passthrough does not imply CUDA libs are available to Claude Science's sandboxed environment.
findings.concern_path: The team may plan local folding/embedding/DL work around the RTX 3090, then discover the Claude Science runtime cannot see `/usr/lib/wsl/lib`, CUDA libraries, devices, or compatible Python wheels from inside bubblewrap.
findings.suggested_action: Promote R6 to a pre-install acceptance gate: run a minimal CUDA operation from inside Claude Science before committing to local-GPU workflows.

findings.title: Detached serve is underpowered for a week-long critical service
findings.evidence: Artifact section 5 uses `claude-science serve --port 8765 --no-browser --detached`; open question 3 asks whether a systemd user service is needed; R3 only covers Windows Terminal crashes.
findings.concern_path: A detached process may survive a terminal crash but not WSL shutdown, Windows reboot, sleep/resume, crash, port conflict, or silent daemon exit. The user may return to a dead endpoint without logs or auto-restart during the hackathon.
findings.suggested_action: Treat persistence as required: define logs, health check, restart behavior, and whether systemd user service support exists in this distro.

findings.title: The HDD placement rule is too coarse for model weights and active scientific workloads
findings.evidence: Artifact section 3 says bulk data and large model weights go to D: HDD via `/mnt/d`, while active scratch stays on C:. Live `Get-PSDrive` shows C: has about 580,646,354,944 bytes free and D: about 1,992,622,116,864 bytes free, so C: has substantial headroom.
findings.concern_path: Large model weights are often repeatedly mmap'ed, decompressed, indexed, or randomly read at job startup. Putting them on a SATA HDD through drvfs can turn every experiment restart into a bottleneck and make the RTX 3090 wait on storage.
findings.suggested_action: Classify data by access pattern: keep active model/cache/worksets on C: or native ext4 storage, and reserve `/mnt/d` for cold/archive or truly sequential reads.

findings.title: The `drvfs` and bubblewrap claim is asserted too strongly without a targeted smoke test
findings.evidence: Artifact section 3 says the bubblewrap sandbox `will not run on /mnt/d (drvfs)`; section 6 only tests `bwrap --ro-bind / / --unshare-all echo ok`, not a project/cache/data path on `/mnt/d`.
findings.concern_path: The team may avoid useful D: layouts because of an overbroad statement, or worse, place data on D: and later hit path, permission, symlink, case-sensitivity, mmap, or inotify problems that the proposed smoke test never covers.
findings.suggested_action: Add explicit smoke tests for Claude Science reading, writing, symlinking, and using a sample project/cache path on `/mnt/d`.

findings.title: The WSL memory change is only partially verified; the applied runtime limit could not be confirmed
findings.evidence: Host file probe confirmed `C:\Users\wijesingheds\.wslconfig` contains `memory=32GB`, `processors=16`, `swap=16GB`, `autoMemoryReclaim=gradual`; backup exists with `memory=8GB`, `processors=4`, `swap=4GB`. But `wsl -d Ubuntu -- free -h` was blocked with `Wsl/Service/E_ACCESSDENIED`, so section 4's `free -h now shows 31 GiB` was not verifiable here.
findings.concern_path: If WSL has not restarted under the new config, or if managed policy prevents access, the plan proceeds assuming memory headroom that may not exist. Docker and GPU jobs could still hit the old 8 GB ceiling or fail before launch.
findings.suggested_action: Confirm `free -h` and `nproc` from the actual user shell after WSL access is restored, not just the host config file.

findings.title: Physical disk topology claims could not be live-verified because Windows storage instrumentation is access-denied
findings.evidence: `Get-PhysicalDisk | Select DeviceId,MediaType,BusType,Size` returned `MethodInvocationException ... Access denied`; `Get-CimInstance Win32_LogicalDisk` also returned `Access denied`. `Get-PSDrive` did verify free space: C: free 580,646,354,944 bytes; D: free 1,992,622,116,864 bytes. Hardware-reference.md says C: is Micron 3400 NVMe and D: is Seagate ST2000DM008 SATA HDD.
findings.concern_path: The plan's SSD/HDD logic is probably right based on the hardware doc, but the live CLAIM is not actually confirmed under current permissions. If drive letters or storage policy changed, the placement decision could be wrong.
findings.suggested_action: Use an approved/elevated inventory source or existing signed hardware report to re-confirm disk media type before making irreversible placement choices.

findings.title: Rollback is incomplete for profile, package, daemon, and data mutations
findings.evidence: Artifact section 8 says restore `.wslconfig`, uninstall per tool docs or delete `~/.local` install dir plus profile entry, and delete D: directory. Section 5 also installs apt packages and modifies shell profile via installer.
findings.concern_path: If the installer edits `.profile`, creates services, auth tokens, caches, logs, browser state, or package directories outside `~/.local`, the documented rollback will leave residue. On a managed machine, residue can matter for support and repeat installs.
findings.suggested_action: Define a pre/post file inventory and uninstall checklist covering profile changes, apt packages, services, auth/cache dirs, logs, and D: data.

findings.title: The checked-in plan appears mojibaked, which is a documentation and review-quality risk
findings.evidence: Reading `docs/claude-science-wsl-install-plan_2026-07-07.md` showed corrupted characters such as `Date: 2026-07-07 ú Machine`, arrows rendered as ``, `≥` rendered as `ò`, and `≠` rendered as `?`.
findings.concern_path: A plan that will be followed under time pressure should not contain ambiguous symbols in requirements, comparisons, or commands. Mojibake also suggests the file may have been edited with inconsistent encoding, which can hurt later review artifacts.
findings.suggested_action: Normalize the document to UTF-8 or ASCII and replace symbolic arrows/comparators with plain text before using it as an operational runbook.

convergence_status: round 1 — no prior position to converge with
recommended_next_question: Can Claude prove, with current official Anthropic documentation and a live user-shell transcript from this managed machine, that Claude Science is available to this account and that `wsl -d Ubuntu` can run the installer and a minimal sandboxed CUDA task?
